coredata.dc:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots loaded magnetite nanoparticles as a bimodal nanoprobe for both fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yan
Li, Long
Zhang, Donghui
Gan, Liping
Zhao, Pei
Zhang, Yinfeng
Zhang, Qi
Hua, Mingqing
Jia, Chunman
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, it is highly desired to develop dual-modal fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (FI/MRI) probes in medical imaging because it unites the respective advantages of each imaging modality: high sensitivity of FI and superior spatial resolution of MRI. In this study, a facile strategy to fabricate a new bimodal imaging nanoprobe (Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4) was reported by integrating the fluorescence ability of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and T 1 and T 2 contrast-enhancing functionality of Gd(III) ions and Fe3O4 nanoparticles into a single hybrid nanostructure. The hybrid composites were investigated by FT-IR, XRD, TEM, XPS, VSM, and so on, which confirmed that Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were successfully obtained and exhibited superparamagnetic property at room temperature. The derived nanoprobes presented an excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior. In addition, r 1 and r 2 relaxivities of the synthesized imaging nanoprobes were measured to be 5.16 and 115.6 mM− 1 s− 1, which nominated Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanocomposites as a suitable T 1-T 2 contrast agent. The Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles combining two synergetic imaging modalities showed great potential in FI/MRI dual-modal imaging for a more complementary and accurate detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-modal imaging
Gd-doped carbon quantum dots
Fe3O4
Fluorescence
Relaxivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of amino functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (a) Fe3O4, (b) N-Fe3O4, (c) Gd-CQDs and (d) Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4.
XRD patterns of GdCl3, (a) Gd-CQDs, (b) Fe3O4 and (c) Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4.
TEM images and the corresponding particle size-distribution histograms of (a–b) Gd-CQDs, (c–d) N-Fe3O4 and (e–f) Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4.
XPS spectra of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4: (a) survey of the sample, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) Gd 4d, (f) Gd 3d and (g) Fe 2p.
Magnetization curves of (a) Fe3O4 and (b) Gd-CQDs@N–Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs, Gd-CQDs and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs, Gd-CQDs and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 excited at 400 nm. (c and d) Fluorescence emission spectra and the respective normalized spectra of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 at different excitation wavelengths.
1/T 1 vs. Gd concentration (a) and 1/T 2 vs. Fe concentration (b) plots of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles at 0.5 T and 32 °C.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 as a dual-modal imaging probe.

head.ce:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots loaded magnetite nanoparticles as a bimodal nanoprobe for both fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pathology, School of Medicine, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD 21205, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pathology, School of Medicine, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, Maryland 21205, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Provincial Key Lab of Fine Chemistry, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Provincial Key Lab of Fine Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hainan Provincial Key Lab of Fine Chemistry, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, it is highly desired to develop dual-modal fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (FI/MRI) probes in medical imaging because it unites the respective advantages of each imaging modality: high sensitivity of FI and superior spatial resolution of MRI. In this study, a facile strategy to fabricate a new bimodal imaging nanoprobe (Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4) was reported by integrating the fluorescence ability of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and T 1 and T 2 contrast-enhancing functionality of Gd(III) ions and Fe3O4 nanoparticles into a single hybrid nanostructure. The hybrid composites were investigated by FT-IR, XRD, TEM, XPS, VSM, and so on, which confirmed that Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were successfully obtained and exhibited superparamagnetic property at room temperature. The derived nanoprobes presented an excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior. In addition, r 1 and r 2 relaxivities of the synthesized imaging nanoprobes were measured to be 5.16 and 115.6 mM− 1 s− 1, which nominated Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanocomposites as a suitable T 1-T 2 contrast agent. The Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles combining two synergetic imaging modalities showed great potential in FI/MRI dual-modal imaging for a more complementary and accurate detection.

ce:section.ce:para: The advent of molecular imaging technologies provides great opportunities for the early and precise diagnosis of malignant diseases. Various molecular imaging modalities, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), ultrasound, fluorescence, X-ray computed tomography (CT), and positron emission tomography (PET), have been used as an effective tool for disease detection in clinic [1–3]. Nevertheless, each imaging mode has its own advantages and intrinsic deficiency. For instance, MRI offers deep tissue penetration and high quality three-dimensional images with excellent spatial resolution in a noninvasive manner, but suffers from low sensitivity [4]. In contrast, fluorescence imaging (FI), another common imaging method, provides single-cell sensitivity and subcellular resolution. However, the utility of this imaging technique is restricted by poor tissue penetration and spatial resolution, which makes it impossible to produce sufficient anatomical details and only suitable for small animal imaging [5]. Apparently, a combination of MRI and FI techniques is highly desirable to bridge gaps in resolution, sensitivity, and tissue penetration between these two modalities, and thus providing more complementary and accurate information for early diagnosis [6,7]. Therefore, exploring bimodal MRI/FI imaging agents with both magnetic and fluorescent property becomes an urgent priority.

With the rapid development in nanotechnology, hybrid nanomaterials uniting fluorescence and MRI modalities have received substantial attention [8–10]. Among all the fluorescent nanostructures, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as one of the excellent framework to construct bimodal MRI/FI nanoprobes, because of their unique electronic and luminescent properties [11,12]. Unfortunately, the heavy metals composition of semiconductor QDs leads to high toxicity and potential pollution hazard, which limits their further biological and medical applications [13]. Hereby, it is favorable to develop new nontoxic metal-free nanomaterials to overcome the drawbacks of traditional QDs. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gathered widespread notice owing to their exceptional optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and green synthesis together with the ease of surface functionalization [14]. The application of CQDs in white LEDs [15], the selective detection of metal ions [16], electrochemical energy storage [17] and biological imaging [18] has been reported so far. These above-mentioned advantages also make CQDs a superior platform to fabricate bimodal imaging nanoprobes by incorporating or doping the magnetic functionality. In fact, CQDs doped with different metal ions have been obtained, such as gadolinium-doped CQDs [19–21], copper-doped CQDs [22] and zinc-doped CQDs [23], which not only possess excellent optical properties but also endow the new functionality.

Among these metallic dopants, gadolinium (Gd) exhibits excellent contrast effect in T 1-weighted MR imaging based on its unique magnetic property, which is always deemed to be suitable for the design of bimodal imaging. In addition, many Gd-containing chelates have been commonly used as effective T 1-positive contrast agents, which resulting in a brighter signal and enhanced image contrast [24,25]. Most importantly, encapsulating Gd ions into nano-payload can effectively suppress Gd leakage while retaining the T 1-shortening capacity [26,27]. On the other hand, superparamagnetic ferroferric oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles are known as negative MRI contrast agents, which inducing magnetic field perturbations and producing a darker signal in the T 2-wighted MR imaging [28]. Compared to T 1 contrast agents, Fe3O4 nanoparticles might confront with ambiguities derived from magnetic susceptibility artifacts, whereas they own better biocompatibility, substantial accumulation at the diseased site and high sensitivity with an excellent contrast-to-noise ratio [29,30]. From literature review, coupling Fe3O4 nanoparticles with CQDs has also been readily achieved [31,32]. In view of this, a synergistic integration of T 1 and T 2 contrast-enhanced imaging as MRI modality into a single hybrid nano-vesicle is beneficial to overcome their individual limitations and potentially provide more comprehensive imaging information.

In this study, a dual-modal imaging agent Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles was synthesized by covalently linking Gd-doped CQDs (Gd-CQDs) with amino functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (N-Fe3O4). The structures, morphologies and fluorescence properties were investigated in detail. The in vitro MRI performance of the obtained Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 materials was also evaluated by T 1 and T 2 relaxometric properties.

Citrate acid, ethanediamine, Gd2O3, N-Hydroxysuxxinimide (NHS, 98%) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-aminoethyl-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (AEAPS) and tetramethyl ammonium hydroxide pentahydrate (TMAH) were purchased from Aladdin Shanghai Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GdCl3 was obtained by dissolving Gd2O3 in hydrochloric acid at an elevated temperature, followed by evaporating the solvent. Other reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification.

Gd-CQDs were prepared according to a previous CQDs method described in the literature with some modifications [33]. Briefly, 1.05 g citrate acid, 0.5 mL ethanediamine, and 0.21 g GdCl3 were dissolved into 10 mL distilled water with ultrasonic mixing for 20 min. Then the solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. Upon cooling to room temperature naturally, the dark brown solution was dialyzed against distilled water using dialysis membrane (MWCO:1000 Da) for 24 h. The residual suspension was finally freeze-dried to give Gd-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amino functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized through a modified solvothermal method [34]. Amino-functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (N-Fe3O4) were prepared by surface functionalization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles using AEAPS as the silylation agent [35,36]. Typically, 2.0 mL toluene solution of as-synthesized Fe3O4 was mixed with 1.0 mL toluene solution with 0.01 mol/L AEAPS. Next, 1.0 mL methanol solution of TMAH (0.01 M) was introduced with continuous stirring for 10 min at 80 °C until complete precipitation. Subsequently, the supernatant was discarded, and the resultant solid precipitate was mixed with 3.0 mL toluene and 1.0 mL ethanol solution of TMAH (0.01 M). The mixture solution was heated to 80 °C and kept at the temperature for 20 min. The obtained precipitate was washed repeatedly in toluene and ethanol and collected after drying at 50 °C under vacuum.

To conjugate Gd-CQDs with N-Fe3O4, the classic carbodiimide crosslinking chemistry between carboxyl groups of CQDs and amine groups of N-Fe3O4 was utilized [37]. In brief, 80.0 mg EDC and 10.0 mg NHS were added to 5.0 mL Gd-CQDs solution (10 mg/mL in PBS buffer) and stirred for 30 min in the dark. Then, 1.0 mg N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were added, followed by stirring gently for 24 h to conjugate with Gd-CQDs. Afterwards, the generated composites were collected with centrifugation separation, and then redispersed in PBS solution for further usage. The preparation of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The transmission electron micrograph of the synthesized samples were investigated by a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transformation infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet Nexus 470 FTIR spectrometer on KBr pellets over the range of 4000–500 cm− 1. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a Shimadzu XRD-6100 X-ray diffractometer equipped with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). The elements on the surface of the obtained materials was determined by the PHI5300 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a monochromatic Mg Kα source. The magnetic properties of samples were detected on a SQUID vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) in the range of −20000 to 20000 Oe at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared samples were measured by a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer.

The quantum yields (QYs) of the synthesized CQDs, Gd-CQDs and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 were obtained by a relative measurement method [38]. The quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was used as a known reference standard with a quantum yield of 0.54 at 360 nm. A linear fitting of the integral fluorescence emission spectral areas and the corresponding absorption value (less than 0.15) was plotted. The QY was calculated according to the following equation:
QY x
=
QY R
Grad x
Grad R
η x 2
η R 2
where the subscripts R and x denoted the reference standard and obtained sample, respectively. QY is the fluorescence quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

Longitudinal relaxation times (T 1) and transverse relaxation time (T 2) of serial dilutions of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 were measured on a 0.5 T MRI instrument (Suzhou Niumag Corporation, Suzhou, China). The Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 samples were dispersed in a 1% aqueous solution of agarose with various concentrations ranging from 0.0012 to 0.384 mM for Gd ions, and from 0.015 to 0.465 for Fe ions, respectively. A standard inversion recovery pulse sequence was applied to measure T 1. T 2 measurements were performed by using a Carr-Purcell-Meiboom-Gill (CPMG) pulse sequence. The relaxivities of r 1 and r 2 were calculated from the plot of T 1 − 1 and T 2 − 1 vs. concentrations of Gd or Fe.

The structural information of the obtained materials was firstly revealed by FT-IR analysis. Fig. 1 displayed the FT-IR spectra of hybrid nanomaterials formation. It was seen from Fig. 1a that Fe3O4 nanoparticles showed the characteristic FeO peak at 586 cm− 1. Successful amino-functionalization of Fe3O4 was evidenced by the absorption peaks at 3430, 1633, 1415 cm− 1, which corresponded to the NH stretching and bending vibration of amine bond (Fig. 1b). Meanwhile, the peak at 1045 cm− 1 belonged to the stretching vibration of SiO bond. FT-IR spectrum of Gd-CQDs in Fig. 1c suggested the peaks at 1697 cm− 1 and 1396 cm− 1 were attributed to CO and C-OH stretching vibration of carboxyl groups. The broad band centered at 3370 cm− 1 was derived from the stretching mode of –OH. The results demonstrated that these oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carbonyl and carboxyl groups, on the edge of the carbon backbone are the common origin for the fluorescence in the CQDs [39]. In addition, the peaks located at 1652 cm− 1 and 1558 cm− 1 belonged to the stretching modes of CC elementary unit in the carbon core and bending vibration of NH. After Gd-CQDs binding with N-Fe3O4, a new characteristic band appearing at 1641 cm− 1 (Fig. 1d) attributed to vibration of –NH–CO–, which verified the successful formation of amide groups in Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4.

XRD analysis was employed to further characterize the formation of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 and the results were shown in Fig. 2 . For the Gd-CQDs, a broad amorphous peak centered at 24.8° (Fig. 2a) was attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms, which was indicative of poor crystalline nature of the prepared Gd-CQDs [33,40]. Compared the XRD spectrum of Gd-CQDs with that of the used GdCl3 precursor, no other diffraction peak belonging to free Gd(Ш) ions could be detected in the XRD patterns of Gd-CQDs, implying that Gd(Ш) was stably coordinated with CQDs. With respect to the Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 2b), the characteristic diffraction peaks with 2θ at 30.2°, 35.6°, 43.2°, 53.6°, 57.2°, 62.7° could be assigned to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) lattice planes, respectively. These diffraction peaks matched well in position and relative intensity with the standard XRD date of pristine Fe3O4 with a cubic inverse spinel structure (JCPDS No. 19-0629) [41]. After the conjugation of Fe3O4 and Gd-CQDs, the presence of a broad diffraction peak at 24.1° in comparison to the XRD spectrum of Fe3O4 was indicative of the presence of Gd-CQDs in the hybrid structure (Fig. 2c). The XRD results confirmed the co-existence of Gd-CQDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles in Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 matrices.

To confirm the morphology of the obtained Gd-CQDs, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4, TEM micrographs and TEM-based size distribution histograms were shown in Fig. 3 . It was evident that the Gd-CQDs were discrete each other with a quasi-spherical shape and the average particle size was 2.25 ± 0.54 nm (Fig. 3a–b). The prepared N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were nearly spherical in shape with some aggregation and the average size was 11.76 ± 2.19 nm (Fig. 3c–d). The morphology and particle size (11.10 ± 2.43 nm) of the obtained Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 (Fig. 3e–f) were almost the same as that of N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles. In addition, there was no obvious Gd-CQDs could be found in the hybrid materials due to the high dispersion and small size of the as-received Gd-CQDs. However, the results of FTIR and XRD analysis have indicated the successful synthesis of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4.

The surface chemical composition of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 was further probed via XPS. As shown in Fig. 4a, the C, N, O, Fe, Gd and Si elements could be observed in the survey scan spectrum of the as-produced Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 with 34.0% C, 6.58% N, 36.02% O, 0.88% Gd, 15.8% Fe and 2.92% Si. The unexpected Na (0.96%), P (2.18%), K (0.21%) and Cl (0.46%) elements came from the PBS preserving liquid of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4. The ratio of Gd-CQDs per Fe3O4 nanoparticle estimated from elemental analysis was 0.06, indicating the low concentration of the as-synthesized Gd-CQDs in the hybrid materials. From the perspective of the synthetic method, the successful formation of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 was achieved via the coupling of carboxyl groups on the surface of Gd-CQDs with amine functionality in the structure of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. It was reported that a high density of tiny CQDs in the environment provided by the carboxyl-amine interactions was facilitated to suppress the agglomeration between Fe3O4 nanoparticles [31]. Consequently, a low density of CQDs could not effectively control the inter-particle fusion of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which was also illustrated from the TEM image of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 with some agglomeration. In the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b), the three component peaks after deconvolution with binding energies of 284.8, 286.0 and 287.8 eV revealed the presence of CC, C-O/C-N and C=O/COOH functional groups, respectively [33]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) showed two components with the binding energies of about 526.0 and 531.2 eV for CO and CO bonds, respectively [42]. Concerning the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d), one main peak at 400.1 eV was attributed to graphitic N group [43]. The Gd 4d XPS spectrum (Fig. 4e) exhibited Gd 4d5/2 and Gd 4d3/2 lines at 142.6 and 148.1 eV. Again, the XPS Gd3d spectrum (Fig. 4f) displayed Gd 3d5/2 line at 1187.0 eV. And it was worth noting that higher binding energy was observed in comparison to the corresponding elemental Gd 4d (141.2 eV) and Gd 3d5/2 signals (1186.0 eV) of Gd3+ in Gd2O3, which was probably related to the different coordination environments [38]. The presence of both Gd 4d and Gd 3d peaks indicated that the oxidation state of Gd remained +3 in Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 hybrid materials [44]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of Fe 2p (Fig. 4g) consisted of the doublet Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 with binding energy values of 710.6 and 724.2 eV, respectively, which matched well with the oxidation state of Fe in Fe3O4 [45]. All the above results strongly validated the successful Gd doping and the expected composition of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in the Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 matrix.

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were determined at room temperature using VSM and the magnetization hysteresis loops are shown in Fig. 5 . It was revealed that the magnetization curves appeared S-shaped over the applied magnetic field and these two samples exhibited superparamagnetic behavior. Their saturation magnetization (Ms) values of pristine Fe3O4 and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were found to be 63.3 and 59.4 emu/g, respectively. The decrease in Ms value probably explained by the can be existence of diamagnetic CQDs contribution and the disturbance of Gd doping on the long-range order of magnetic spins in Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles [46]. Although Ms value was lower than that of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanoparticles would meet the basic requirements for using as an MRI contrast agent in biomedical field [47].

The optical properties of the CQDs, Gd-CQDs and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 were illustrated with UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 6a, the obtained Gd-CQDs had the similar UV–Vis absorption spectrum to CQDs. The absorption peaks, including a shoulder peak at 240 nm and a relatively strong absorption characteristic peak at 340 nm, were clearly observed. These two peaks were attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of conjugated CC units within the carbon core and n-π* transition of CO band, respectively [16,48]. Inconsistent with the CQDs and Gd-CQDs, a broad absorption band with no noticeable peak along the whole region from 200 to 800 nm was presented in the UV–Vis spectrum of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 due to the total absorption by Fe3O4. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs, Gd-CQDs and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 excited at 400 nm were shown in Fig. 6b. It was apparently seen that there were no any emission wavelength shift between the as-prepared CQDs and Gd-CQDs, which suggested the fluorescence emission properties of CQDs were remained in the hybrid material. However, the fluorescence intensity of Gd-CQDs was slightly weakened in comparison to original CQDs, because Gd3+ disrupted the radiative recombination of the CQDs [38]. Compared to the CQDs and Gd-CQDs, the maximum emission peak of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 was slightly red-shifted by ~8 nm, suggesting the successful conjugation of Fe3O4 with Gd-CQDs. Additionally, Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 presented dramatic fluorescence intensity decline, hinting the electron transfer between Gd-CQDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles [31]. The relative quantum yield of CQDs, Gd-CQDs and Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 investigated using quinine bisulfate as a reference were 48.32%, 31.78% and 1.29%, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra and the respective normalized spectra of CQDs and Gd-CQDs were provided in Fig. S1. Both CQDs and Gd-CQDs displayed a characteristic excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior owing to the different surface energy trap distribution [40,49]. When the excitation wavelength was varied from 310 to 410 nm, the emission peak position gradually red-shifted to longer wavelength. Moreover, the fluorescence emission spectra and the respective normalized spectra of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 with progressively increased excitation wavelengths were also recorded and shown in Fig. 6(c–d). It was observed that the emission peak position was almost invariable as the excitation wavelength increased from 280 to 410 nm, which may be associated with the effect of different surface atoms [50]. The maximum emission of the as-received Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 could be obtained with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm.

In order to validate the potential application of the Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 hybrid nanoparticles as an effective multi-modal MRI contrast agent, their magnetic relaxation properties were investigated. For the quantitative evaluation, the specific relaxivities of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4, r 1 and r 2, were determined by taking the slope of the linear plot of relaxation rates (1/T 1 and 1/T 2) vs. the concentration of the Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4. Fig. 7a showed the r 1 relaxivity of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 against the Gd concentration (measured by ICP-AES). Obviously, r 1 value of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 was calculated to be 5.16 mM− 1·s− 1, which was higher than commercial contrast agent Gd-DTPA (3.85 mM− 1·s− 1) [24], suggesting that Gd-doping in the hybrids enhanced the T 1 contrast. Fig. 7b showed the r 2 relaxivity of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 against the Fe concentration. It was clearly observed that r 2 value of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 was 115.6 mM− 1·s− 1. The high r 2/r 1 ratio (22.4) of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 hybrid material indicated the dominance of the T 2-shortening effect and dark contrast enhancement. In spite of this, the higher r 1 value of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 compared to the commercial Gd-DTPA would meet the basic requirement for the application in T 1-weighted MR imaging. The results demonstrated that Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanocomposites could be acted as a suitable T 1-T 2 contrast agent.

Furthermore, the obtained Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 hybrid material was compared with those reported in literature (Table S1). The observation throughout this comprehensive table revealed that Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 provided a great potential served as a bimodal nanoprobe for both fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging.

In summary, a promising bimodal imaging nanoprobe Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 was successfully prepared and characterized. The Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 nanocomposites could emit fluorescence and exhibited an excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior owing to the effect of surface chemistry, thus was potential for reliable fluorescence imaging. Meanwhile, the relaxivities of Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 with r 1 = 5.16 mM− 1 s− 1 and r 2 = 115.6 mM− 1 s− 1 suggested its applicability as a T 1-T 2 imaging agent. Benefiting from the effective integration of fluorescence and MRI ability, Gd-CQDs@N-Fe3O4 was considered to be a fluorescence and magnetic resonance dual-modal probe toward improved accuracy and better detection in medical imaging.

Yan Huang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Long Li: Writing - original draft. Donghui Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Liping Gan: Data curation. Pei Zhao: Software, Investigation. Yinfeng Zhang: Validation. Qi Zhang: Visualization. Mingqing Hua: Resources. Chunman Jia: Project administration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21601068) and Scientific Research Foundation for Advanced Talents, Jiangsu University (14JDG054) for the financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mri.2020.02.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu Q, Guo BD, Rao ZY, Zhang BH, Gong JR. Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging. Nano Lett 2013; 13: 2436-2441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inorganic nanoparticles for MRI contrast agents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Na HB, Song IC, Hyeon T. Inorganic nanoparticles for MRI contrast agents. Adv Mater 2009; 21: 2133-2148.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pegylated dendrimer-entrapped gold nanoparticles for in vivo blood pool and tumor imaging by computed tomography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Peng C, Zheng LF, Chen Q, Shen MW, Guo R, Wang H, et al. Pegylated dendrimer-entrapped gold nanoparticles for in vivo blood pool and tumor imaging by computed tomography. Biomaterials 2012; 33: 1107-1119.
